threats.title: Bribe Rewards Permanently Locked for AVM Users
threats.hypothesis_description: Users who delegate their veNFTs to the Automated Voting Manager (AVM) will have all their bribe rewards permanently locked in the AutoVotingEscrow contract.
threats.investigation_rationale: Bribe.getReward attempts to route AVM rewards to the original owner by checking `if(_owner == avm)`. However, `_owner` is the individual AutoVotingEscrow contract, not the manager (`avm`), so the check fails. Furthermore, even if it passed, `avm.originalOwner()` is a no-op that returns address(0). Rewards are sent to the AutoVotingEscrow contract, which lacks any ERC20 withdrawal functions.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Incorrect address comparison and empty originalOwner mapping

preconditions: User enables Auto-Voting
AVM votes for a pool that accrues bribe rewards
User or gaugeManager calls getReward

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Normal protocol usage results in 100% loss of bribe yield for AVM users
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: No direct attacker profit, but massive permanent loss of user funds (yield)

economic_questions: Can the AutoVotingEscrow contract be upgraded to rescue the funds?
Are there any indirect ways to force the AutoVotingEscrow to transfer ERC20s?
validation_requirements: Verify that AutoVotingEscrow has no token recovery mechanism
Confirm that setOriginalOwner is completely empty in the deployed bytecode

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Severe loss of trust and yield for AVM users

potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: All bribe rewards allocated to AVM voting power

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Trace exact ownerOf return value for AVM tokens

threats.title: Delegation System Wiped by Logic Error
threats.hypothesis_description: Any token transfer wipes out the voting power of the source delegatee for all tokens they do not personally own, completely destroying the DAO delegation system.
threats.investigation_rationale: In `VotingDelegationLib.moveTokenDelegates`, the code filters tokens to keep in the source delegate's checkpoint using `if(ownerOfFn(tId) == srcRep)`. `srcRep` is the delegatee, but `ownerOfFn` returns the token owner. Since most delegated tokens are owned by the delegators, not the delegatee, any action triggering this function (like a token transfer) will drop all delegated tokens from the delegatee's checkpoint.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Incorrect ownership check drops delegated tokens

preconditions: Users have delegated veNFTs to a representative
Any token transfer, mint, or burn occurs involving that representative

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Attacker delegates a dust token to a target representative, then transfers it, wiping the representative's entire delegated voting power.
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Attacker can completely control governance by wiping all competing delegates' voting power at will.

economic_questions: Can this be used to manipulate gauge weights or only DAO governance?
Is there any way for delegates to recover their voting power?
validation_requirements: Test delegation of multiple tokens to a single address, followed by a transfer of one token
Verify the resulting checkpoint voting power for the delegatee

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Minimal (enough to create one dust lock)

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: Right before a critical governance snapshot

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Check if moveTokenDelegates is called on every transfer
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Validate the ownerOfFn return value vs srcRep

threats.title: Rebase Rewards Permanently Lost if Checkpoint Delayed
threats.hypothesis_description: If `checkpoint_token` is not called for more than 10 hours, the undistributed rebase tokens for the skipped weeks are permanently locked in the RewardsDistributor contract.
threats.investigation_rationale: The `_checkpoint_token` loop is hardcoded to a maximum of 20 iterations. With `WEEK = 1800` seconds (30 minutes), 20 iterations equal 10 hours. If the time since the last checkpoint exceeds 10 hours, the loop terminates early, updating `last_token_time` to the current timestamp but leaving the remaining fraction of `to_distribute` permanently unallocated.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Hardcoded loop limit truncates reward distribution

preconditions: Minter deposits tokens to RewardsDistributor
checkpoint_token is not called for > 10 hours

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Network congestion, keeper failure, or intentional delay causes the 10-hour window to be missed.
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: No direct profit, but massive permanent loss of rebase yield for all veNFT holders.

economic_questions: How much yield is lost per missed hour beyond the 10-hour limit?
Can the depositor rescue the locked tokens?
validation_requirements: Simulate a 12-hour gap between checkpoints and measure the unallocated token balance
Verify that token_last_balance updates fully, preventing future distribution of the skipped tokens

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: During network outages or high gas periods
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Severe deflationary pressure and loss of user yield

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All veNFT holders expecting rebase rewards
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: All rebase emissions during the delayed period

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Verify WEEK constant is exactly 1800 in production
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Calculate exact token loss formula based on since_last

threats.title: Bribe Claiming DoS via Unbounded Epoch Loop
threats.hypothesis_description: Users who delay claiming their bribe rewards for 1-2 months will be permanently unable to claim them due to an out-of-gas revert caused by an unbounded loop over 30-minute epochs.
threats.investigation_rationale: The `earned` function in `Bribes.sol` iterates from the epoch of `lastEarn` to the current epoch. Because `WEEK` is set to 1800 seconds (30 minutes), there are 48 epochs per day. Waiting 30 days results in 1440 loop iterations, each performing multiple storage reads. This will quickly exceed the block gas limit.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Unbounded loop over short epochs exceeds gas limit

preconditions: User earns bribe rewards
User does not claim rewards for ~30+ days

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: for (uint256 i = 0; i < numEpochs; i++) { ... storage reads ... }
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Natural user behavior (delaying claims to save gas) triggers the DoS.
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: No direct profit, but users permanently lose their earned bribes.

economic_questions: Exactly how many days does it take to hit the Avalanche block gas limit?
Can the rewards be rescued by an admin?
validation_requirements: Calculate gas cost per iteration of the earned() loop
Test claiming after a simulated 30-day delay

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Loss of user funds and trust

potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: All unclaimed bribes older than the gas limit threshold

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Measure exact gas per loop iteration
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Determine exact day threshold for DoS

threats.title: Gauge Emission Sniping via `poke` Bypass
threats.hypothesis_description: Attackers can bypass the protocol's anti-sniping protection (which prevents voting in the last hour of an epoch) by using the `poke` function to massively increase their vote weight at the last second.
threats.investigation_rationale: The `vote` function explicitly reverts if called in the last hour by a non-AVM user (`revert("AVM_W")`). However, `poke`, which re-casts votes with updated balances, lacks this check. An attacker can vote early with a dust balance, then call `increase_amount` (which automatically calls `poke`) in the last hour. This updates their vote weight to a massive new balance during the restricted window, sniping gauge emissions.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Missing epochVoteEnd check allows last-hour voting

preconditions: Attacker has a veNFT with a small initial lock
Attacker votes for a target gauge early in the epoch
Attacker has capital to increase lock amount in the last hour

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: poke() lacks the `_timestamp >= BlackTimeLibrary.epochVoteEnd(_timestamp)` check present in vote()
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Attacker observes all other votes, then injects massive capital in the last hour to guarantee majority emission share for their chosen pool.
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Attacker maximizes LP emissions for their own pools while minimizing capital lock time risk.

economic_questions: How much capital is required to meaningfully shift emissions in the last hour?
Does the short epoch time (30 mins) make this attack highly repeatable?
validation_requirements: Simulate calling increase_amount in the last 5 minutes of the epoch
Verify that gauge weights update successfully despite the anti-sniping window

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Unfair distribution of emissions, defeating the purpose of the AVM window

potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: A significant portion of weekly BLACK emissions

threats.title: Rebase Rewards Locked via `claim_many`
threats.hypothesis_description: Anyone can call `claim_many` on expired AVM-delegated locks to permanently trap the user's rebase rewards in the AutoVotingEscrow contract.
threats.investigation_rationale: The `claim_many` function in `RewardsDistributor` lacks the AVM original owner resolution logic found in the single `claim` function. For expired locks, it sends rewards directly to `ownerOf(_tokenId)`. For AVM-delegated tokens, this is the `AutoVotingEscrow` contract, which has no withdrawal function. An attacker can call `claim_many` to grief AVM users.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Missing AVM owner resolution sends rewards to lock contract

preconditions: User has an AVM-delegated veNFT
The lock has expired (end < block.timestamp)
Rebase rewards are available to claim

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Attacker calls claim_many([target_tokenId]) to force the rewards into the unrecoverable AVM contract.
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: No direct profit, purely a griefing attack causing 100% loss of rebase yield for the victim.

economic_questions: Can the victim front-run the attack by calling claim()?
Are there MEV bots that automatically call claim_many?
validation_requirements: Verify that claim_many does not check AVM ownership
Confirm that AutoVotingEscrow cannot transfer out the received BLACK tokens

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Loss of user funds and trust in AVM

potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: All rebase rewards for expired AVM locks

threats.title: `delegateBySig` Nonce Reuse Allows Delegation Hijacking
threats.hypothesis_description: A previously signed delegation message remains valid indefinitely even if the user manually changes their delegation, allowing an attacker to hijack the user's voting power at a critical moment.
threats.investigation_rationale: The `delegateBySig` function increments the user's nonce, but calling `delegate()` directly does not. If a user signs a delegation message but later changes their mind and calls `delegate()` directly, their nonce remains unchanged. An attacker holding the old signature can submit it at any time to overwrite the user's current delegation.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Manual delegation does not increment nonce

preconditions: User signs a delegateBySig message
User later calls delegate() directly to change their representative
Attacker possesses the original signed message

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: nonce == nonces[signatory]++ is only checked in delegateBySig, not delegate
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Attacker waits for a critical governance vote, then submits the old signature to hijack the victim's voting power.
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Attacker can swing governance votes or extract bribes by controlling the hijacked voting power.

economic_questions: How much voting power can be hijacked this way?
Is the delegateBySig feature actively used by frontends?
validation_requirements: Test signing a delegation, calling delegate() manually, then submitting the signature
Verify that the signature successfully overrides the manual delegation

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Users who utilized off-chain delegation signing

threats.title: Bribe `withdraw` Silently Fails Allowing Phantom Votes
threats.hypothesis_description: If a gauge's internal bribe contract is replaced, users can retain their bribe deposits in the old contract while their votes are cleared, allowing them to double-dip rewards.
threats.investigation_rationale: In `Bribes.sol`, `withdraw` checks `if (amount <= balanceOf[tokenId])` but does not revert if false. If `gaugeManager` replaces a gauge's internal bribe contract, `VoterV3._reset` will call `withdraw` on the NEW bribe contract (which has 0 balance for the user). The withdrawal silently fails, but `VoterV3` clears the user's vote weight. The user retains their deposit in the OLD bribe contract and can vote again to deposit into the NEW one.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Missing revert on insufficient balance allows phantom votes

preconditions: User votes for a pool
Gauge Admin replaces the internal or external bribe contract for that pool
User calls reset() or poke()

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: if (amount <= balanceOf[tokenId]) { ... } with no else revert
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: User exploits the silent failure to clear their VoterV3 state without clearing their Bribe contract state.
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: User earns bribe rewards from both the old and new bribe contracts simultaneously using the same voting power.

economic_questions: Does the old bribe contract continue to receive rewards?
Are there other ways to trigger the silent failure besides gauge replacement?
validation_requirements: Simulate a gauge replacement and subsequent user reset
Verify the user's balance in the old bribe contract remains intact

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Theft of bribe rewards from legitimate voters

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Other voters in the old bribe contract
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Remaining rewards in the old bribe contract

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Check how gaugeManager updates bribe contracts
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Verify VoterV3 state clears despite Bribe withdraw failure

threats.title: SMNFT Bonus Change Corrupts Global Power
threats.hypothesis_description: Changing the SMNFT bonus multiplier retroactively corrupts the global voting power accounting, breaking quorum calculations and reward distribution math.
threats.investigation_rationale: The `setSmNFTBonus` function changes the bonus multiplier globally. Existing SMNFTs retain their old amounts. When these tokens are split or checkpointed, `calculate_original_sm_nft_amount` uses the NEW bonus to reverse-calculate the underlying amount. This causes the sum of individual balances to permanently diverge from the global `totalSupply`, which calculates the bonus dynamically based on `smNFTBalance`.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Global parameter change breaks historical invariant

preconditions: Users have created SMNFT locks
Admin calls setSmNFTBonus to change the multiplier

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: u_new.smNFT = calculate_original_sm_nft_amount(new_locked.amount) uses new rate on old amounts
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Admin action inadvertently corrupts the core accounting invariant of the VotingEscrow contract.
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: No direct attacker profit, but systemic accounting failure leading to incorrect rebase distributions.

economic_questions: How severely does the totalSupply diverge from actual balances?
Does this allow users to extract more rebase rewards than intended?
validation_requirements: Simulate creating an SMNFT, changing the bonus, and splitting the SMNFT
Compare the sum of balanceOfNFT() against totalSupply()

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Check if SMNFT_BONUS is intended to be mutable

threats.title: Unbounded AVM Loop Causes DoS in `getTotalVotingPower`
threats.hypothesis_description: As the protocol grows and multiple AVM instances are filled with locks, the `getTotalVotingPower` function will exceed the block gas limit, breaking any integrations that rely on it.
threats.investigation_rationale: In `AutoVotingEscrowManager.sol`, `getTotalVotingPower` iterates over all AVM instances. Each AVM instance then iterates over all its locks (up to 1024) and calls `balanceOfNFT` for each. With just a few full AVMs, this nested loop will easily exceed the Avalanche block gas limit.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Nested unbounded loops over all AVMs and locks

preconditions: Protocol achieves moderate adoption of the AVM feature
Multiple AVM instances are created and filled with locks

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: for(uint i=0; i<avms.length; i++) { total += avms[i].getTotalVotingPower(); }
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: An attacker could intentionally fill AVMs with dust locks to trigger the DoS prematurely.
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Griefing attack to break third-party integrations or UI components relying on total AVM voting power.

economic_questions: Are there any core protocol state transitions that rely on this view function?
How many locks are required to hit the gas limit?
validation_requirements: Calculate gas cost of a single balanceOfNFT call
Determine the maximum number of locks before DoS occurs

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Moderate (cost to create many dust locks)

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Degraded protocol functionality and UI breakage

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Check if any state-changing functions call getTotalVotingPower
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Gas profiling of the nested loops

threats.title: Fee-on-Transfer Tokens Break Bribe Solvency
threats.hypothesis_description: If a fee-on-transfer token is whitelisted as a connector and used as a bribe, the Bribe contract will record more rewards than it actually holds, leading to insolvency.
threats.investigation_rationale: The `notifyRewardAmount` function in `Bribes.sol` uses the raw `reward` amount passed as an argument to update `tokenRewardsPerEpoch`, rather than calculating the actual balance change after `safeTransferFrom`. If the token deducts a fee on transfer, the contract will owe more tokens to users than it possesses.
deployment_assumptions: A fee-on-transfer token is whitelisted as a connector via TokenHandler
threats.config_evidence: TokenHandler.sol allows governance to whitelist connectors, which Bribes.sol accepts as valid reward tokens.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Uses raw amount instead of balance delta

preconditions: Governance whitelists a fee-on-transfer token as a connector
A user deposits this token as a bribe

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: The contract becomes insolvent. The first users to claim get their full share, while the last users to claim will revert due to insufficient contract balance.
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Early claimers effectively steal yield from late claimers.

economic_questions: Does the protocol intend to support fee-on-transfer tokens?
Can an attacker intentionally use a malicious token to trap other users' claims?
validation_requirements: Verify TokenHandler whitelist policies
Simulate a bribe deposit with a mock fee-on-transfer token

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Loss of user funds and trust

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Late claimers of the bribe token
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: 100% loss of their share of that specific bribe
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: The fee percentage of the total bribe deposit

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Confirm lack of balance delta check

threats.title: PermissionsRegistry `removeRole` Corrupts Tracking
threats.hypothesis_description: Removing a role from the protocol corrupts the role tracking data for all users who held that role, potentially breaking off-chain systems or future on-chain logic.
threats.investigation_rationale: In `PermissionsRegistry.removeRole`, the code attempts to remove the role from each user's `_addressToRoles` array using a swap-and-pop pattern. However, it uses `_roles[_roles.length - 1]` (a role from the global roles array) instead of the last element of the user's specific array. This overwrites the user's role entry with an unrelated global role.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Uses wrong array source for swap-and-pop

preconditions: Admin calls removeRole to delete an existing role from the system
Users exist who were assigned that role

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Admin action inadvertently corrupts the role tracking arrays for all affected users.
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: No direct profit, but breaks protocol access control visibility and off-chain tracking.

economic_questions: Do any on-chain contracts rely on addressToRole() for logic?
How does this affect the frontend UI?
validation_requirements: Simulate removing a role and querying addressToRole for an affected user
Verify the presence of the corrupted role string

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Data corruption and potential operational disruption

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Check usage of addressToRole across the codebase
